critic_raw:
The design uses wearable device data collected over 'the past year' for 10,000 patient records. If the neural network uses time-series sequences from wearable devices and the data spans one year, many patients may have very short or discontinuous wearable records if device adoption varied. The design does not describe the average duration of wearable device wear per patient, the fraction of patients with complete vs. sparse coverage, or how patients who stopped wearing devices before the 30-day readmission window are handled. This missingness is almost certainly non-random and likely correlated with readmission risk (sicker patients may be less compliant with wearables), which would bias both training and evaluation.

The stratified random split on a one-year dataset is less problematic temporally than for multi-year datasets, but a critical patient-level isolation requirement is not mentioned. A patient discharged multiple times within the year could have their admissions split across training and test sets. The neural network would then see the patient's wearable data from one admission during training and predict on a different admission for the same patient — effectively seeing the patient's physiological baseline, which is a form of data leakage that favors the sequence model.

The comparison between a neural network on wearable device time-series and logistic regression on 'clinical demographics' is not a controlled test of the wearable data hypothesis. The logistic regression baseline does not include any wearable features; its inferiority could be due to using a weaker model, using fewer features, or using less informative feature types. A three-arm design — (1) logistic regression on demographics, (2) logistic regression on wearable features, (3) neural network on wearable sequences — is needed to isolate the contribution of the sequence modeling architecture from the information content of wearable data.
defender_raw:
Wearable device time-series data offers a genuinely different information channel from clinical demographics for readmission prediction, and the neural network design correctly exploits this. Wearable data captures post-discharge physiological dynamics — heart rate variability, activity recovery trajectories, sleep disruption — that are invisible to clinical demographic features extracted at discharge. A patient whose heart rate fails to decrease after discharge and whose activity remains suppressed two weeks later is showing a physiological signal of recovery failure that no demographic feature can encode. This is the scientific basis for the hypothesis, and it is well-supported by clinical literature on post-discharge monitoring.

AUROC as the primary metric is appropriate for a risk stratification application. The model's output is used to generate a ranked list of patients for follow-up prioritization, and AUROC measures the quality of this ranking across all possible thresholds. Accuracy and precision as secondary metrics complete the evaluation picture. Socioeconomic status stratification for confound control is particularly thoughtful: SDOH factors that correlate with both wearable device adherence and readmission risk (lower-income patients may have poorer recovery environments and lower device usage compliance) represent a genuine confound that the design explicitly addresses.

The feature aggregation approach — computing statistics over specified time windows from wearable data — is a sensible representation that balances the richness of continuous monitoring data with the need for stable model inputs. Window-based aggregation also makes the model more robust to the missingness patterns inherent in consumer wearable devices, where data gaps of hours are common and raw time-series models would need to handle these explicitly.

The stratified random split is justified by the premise that the primary signal is cross-sectional — post-discharge physiological recovery patterns are driven by patient physiology, not calendar time — and the one-year dataset is appropriate for a single-year post-discharge monitoring study. Equal tuning effort for the logistic regression baseline ensures the comparison tests the informational value of wearable data rather than model sophistication.
all_issues_raised: Wearable coverage completeness is unaddressed; non-random device dropout likely correlated with readmission risk, biasing labels
No patient-level split isolation; multiple admissions per patient can straddle train and test sets, leaking patient physiological baseline to the neural network
Comparison conflates wearable data information value with neural network architectural advantage; no wearable-features logistic regression arm
Dataset size (10,000 records, one year) may be insufficient for stable AUROC estimation at patient subgroup level
Wearable aggregation window sizes are unspecified; choice of aggregation window significantly affects what temporal patterns the model can detect
all_issues_adjudicated: Wearable coverage completeness is unaddressed; non-random device dropout likely correlated with readmission risk, biasing labels
No patient-level split isolation; multiple admissions per patient can straddle train and test sets, leaking patient physiological baseline to the neural network
Comparison conflates wearable data information value with neural network architectural advantage; no wearable-features logistic regression arm
